people.bio: John Renals was an Alderman, Justice of the Peace, Sheriff of Nottingham (1875–76) and Mayor of Nottingham (1889–90). He was the twin brother of Sir Joseph Renals, 1st Baronet. The brothers initially made their fortune in the bleaching business. John took the counterpart lease on 29 Cavendish Road East (Gartree House) on 24th March 1884. His sister Maria Renals married solicitor Parker Woodward and lived at 23 and then 25 Cavendish Road East.

Sir Joseph Renals, 1st Baronet (d. 1 November 1907) was a highly successful City of London trader and public figure. He was the twin brother of John Renals of Nottingham. The brothers initially made their fortune in the bleaching business.

Sir Joseph became a Director of numerous companies including Brewers' and General Fire Insurance and Guarantee Co., Northern Assets Corporation, Northern Debenture Corporation, Pahang Exploration and Development Company, Economic Gold Extraction Company, British Commercial Corporation, Baltimore Breweries Company, and Byers Patents. He was active in Freemasonry as a Senior Deacon in the Aldersgate Lodge, and a member of the Reform Club, the Devonshire Club, the City Liberal Club, and the Aldersgate Club.

He joined the Corporation of London as a Common Councillor for Aldersgate ward in 1885. From 1888 until his death in 1907 he was an Alderman. He was Sheriff of London in 1892 and Lord Mayor of London in 1894–95. He was made a Baronet in 1895.

William Henry Raven was an engineer who joined Raleigh Cycles as Works Manager in 1907 and eventually became Works Director, retiring in 1944 (although he worked elsewhere between 1922 and 1929). Working alongside Sir Harold Bowden (son of founder Sir Frank Bowden), Raven was largely responsible for the firm's modernisation and the massive expansion of its manufacturing capacity during the 1920s and 1930s. He was awarded an O.B.E. in 1942.

A 1931 patent application lists him as residing at 45 Lenton Road. He filed a number of cycling-related patents including: 3rd October 1931, 'improvements in and relating to variable speed gear hubs for bicycles and the like', with Sturmey-Archer Gears Ltd; and 21st September 1932, 'improvements relating to cycle gear cases', with Raleigh Cycle Co. Ltd.

Raven took the assignment of 29 Cavendish Road East from H.V. Turner on 6th December 1932 and remained there until he assigned the property to H.L. Bamkin on 17th August 1948.

Harry Lawrence Bamkin (b. c.1902) was possibly the son of Harry Hibbert Bamkin, a hosiery manufacturer in Hucknall. He received the assignment of 29 Cavendish Road East (Gartree House) from William Henry Raven on 17th August 1948, becoming the tenant. In 1950, a Deed of Variation gave retrospective approval to his subdivision of the house into two dwellings. He subsequently purchased the freehold from the University of Oxford on 9th July 1953 for £1,400.

occupations.occupation: Tenant and later freeholder, 29 Cavendish Road East
occupations.notes: Subdivided property into two dwellings c.1950; purchased freehold July 1953 for £1,400

census_entries.notes: Possibly listed in Hucknall household of Harry Hibbert Bamkin, hosiery manufacturer

significant_places.name: Raleigh Cycles, Nottingham
significant_places.description: Raleigh Cycles was founded by Frank Bowden and became one of the world's leading bicycle manufacturers. William Henry Raven of 29 Cavendish Road East served as Works Manager (from 1907) and Works Director, overseeing massive expansion of manufacturing capacity in the 1920s and 1930s.

person_connections.connection: Works Manager 1907, Works Director, retired 1944; O.B.E. 1942 for services to the firm

significant_places.name: Mansion House, London
significant_places.description: The official residence of the Lord Mayor of London. Sir Joseph Renals served as Lord Mayor of London in 1894–95.

person_connections.connection: Lord Mayor of London 1894–95; made Baronet 1895
